## [45:38 - 47:27]

accomplish when you go in and do this, right? We really quickly look through a bunch of things that give me a bunch of jumping off points for what I would go test and what I would try. But at the end of the day, you need to be committing dedicated time to looking at what is working and what is not working. That is what analysis can do for you. And that is how you get that compounding interest. So what I like to recommend people do is pick one day a week that every week you're going to go in and spend 20 minutes, right? 20 to 30 minutes jumping in and looking at your data. You're going to go look at your open rates for the past week, see which posts seem like they got a lot of engagement. You're going to look at your traffic sources by content. You're going to look at which ones drove revenue. You're going to figure out what content types seem to respond. And then you want to pick a day every month that you do this and a day every quarter that you do this because larger periods of time are actually better to understand trends. But looking weekly is really great to say, wow, this thing had lightning response. How do we do more with it? Or maybe this is the worst post we've ever sent out in our lives. Like I don't want to email this to the audience. Let's email some old content that worked really well because people did not engage.
